turns.text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

> **Re:** *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC*, Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.) --- Proposed Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan

**cc:** Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel, Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc.

Dear Ms. Dominguez:

Thank you for the continued confidence that Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. (\"Pinnacle\") has placed in Hargrove & Linden LLP (the \"Firm\") in connection with the above-captioned matter. As you know, Pinnacle engaged the Firm in September 2024 to prosecute claims against MedCore Analytics, LLC (\"MedCore\") arising from the departure of three former Pinnacle data scientists and the alleged misappropriation of Pinnacle\'s proprietary predictive-analytics algorithms and related confidential information. The claims asserted by Pinnacle include breach of contract (specifically, violations of non-disclosure and non-competition agreements), misappropriation of trade secrets under both the Georgia Trade Secrets Act of 1990 (O.C.G.A. § 10-1-760 *et seq.*) and the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (18 U.S.C. § 1836 *et seq.*), and unjust enrichment. MedCore, represented by Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, has asserted counterclaims for tortious interference with business relations and a declaratory judgment of non-infringement. The amount in controversy is approximately \$47.5 million in claimed damages, and the matter is presided over by the Honorable Richard W. Stanton.

Discovery in this matter commenced on February 3, 2025, pursuant to the Court\'s Scheduling Order, and the discovery close deadline is September 30, 2025. Given the scope and complexity of the anticipated discovery --- which will include voluminous document productions relating to proprietary algorithm source code and internal communications, depositions of the three former Pinnacle data scientists now employed by MedCore, extensive written interrogatories and requests for production, and likely contested motions to compel --- the Firm believes it is prudent and necessary to submit a comprehensive staffing plan for the discovery phase (February through September 2025).

This letter is submitted in accordance with Pinnacle\'s Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (January 2023 version) (the \"Billing Guidelines\"), specifically **Section 4.2**, which requires that outside counsel obtain pre-approval of all timekeepers assigned to a matter and that any subsequent changes to the staffing plan receive written pre-approval from Pinnacle\'s designated in-house counsel. **[PINNACLE HEALTH SYSTEMS, INC.]{.underline}**

**[OUTSIDE COUNSEL BILLING GUIDELINES]{.underline}**

**Effective Date: January 1, 2023**

Issued by the Office of the General Counsel

Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. 2800 Peachtree Road NE, Suite 1400 Atlanta, GA 30305

These Guidelines are binding on all outside counsel engaged by Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. and its subsidiaries (collectively, \"Pinnacle\" or the \"Company\"). Acceptance of any engagement from Pinnacle --- whether by execution of an engagement letter, submission of an invoice, commencement of billable work, or any other action constituting performance of legal services on behalf of the Company --- constitutes agreement to comply with these Guidelines in their entirety. Outside Counsel is expected to familiarize all timekeepers assigned to Pinnacle matters with the contents of these Guidelines and to ensure full compliance at every level of the engagement team. Failure to comply with these Guidelines may result in reductions, rejections, or other remedies as set forth herein. Questions regarding the interpretation or application of these Guidelines should be directed to the Designated In-House Counsel for the applicable matter or to the Office of the General Counsel.

**[SECTION 1 --- PURPOSE AND SCOPE]{.underline}**

**1.1 Purpose.** These Guidelines establish the policies and procedures governing the retention, billing, and payment of outside counsel retained by Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. (\"Pinnacle\" or the \"Company\"), a Delaware corporation with principal offices in Atlanta, Georgia. The purpose of these Guidelines is to ensure cost-effective, transparent, and accountable legal representation across all matters handled by Outside Counsel on behalf of Pinnacle and its subsidiaries. Pinnacle operates fourteen (14) acute-care hospitals and affiliated physician practices across the southeastern United States, generating annual revenue of approximately \$2.8 billion. The Company maintains an internal legal department of twelve (12) attorneys under the leadership of the General Counsel. Pinnacle\'s litigation portfolio typically includes eight to twelve (8--12) active matters at any given time, with annual outside counsel expenditures of approximately \$6.5 million. Given the magnitude and recurring nature of this spend, disciplined cost management, predictable budgeting, and rigorous adherence to billing standards are essential to the Company\'s stewardship of corporate resources.

**1.2 Scope and Applicability**

These Guidelines apply to all law firms, attorneys, legal professionals, contract attorneys, staff attorneys, legal process outsourcing providers, and other legal service providers (collectively, \"Outside Counsel\") engaged by Pinnacle on any matter, whether litigation, regulatory, transactional, advisory, or otherwise. Outside Counsel\'s acceptance of an engagement letter, submission of an invoice, or performance of work on behalf of Pinnacle constitutes acceptance of and agreement to comply with these Guidelines in their entirety. In the event of a conflict between these Guidelines and an engagement letter or fee agreement, the engagement letter or fee agreement controls only to the extent the conflict is expressly identified therein and the deviation is set forth with specificity; in all other respects, these Guidelines govern. These Guidelines supersede all prior versions of Pinnacle\'s billing guidelines and any prior oral or written representations regarding billing practices. The General Counsel, currently Sandra Whitmore, or her designee has the authority to grant written exceptions to any provision of these Guidelines on a case-by-case basis.

**1.3 Amendments.** Pinnacle reserves the right to amend these Guidelines at any time. Amendments will be communicated in writing to all active Outside Counsel and will apply to all invoices submitted after the effective date of the amendment. Continued performance of work or submission of invoices following receipt of an amendment constitutes acceptance of the amended Guidelines.

**[SECTION 2 --- DEFINITIONS]{.underline}**

The following terms, when used in these Guidelines with initial capitalization, shall have the meanings set forth below:

**\"Administrative Task\"** means a task of a non-legal, clerical, or logistical nature, including but not limited to: scheduling meetings and depositions, coordinating with court reporters or vendors, arranging travel or accommodation, filing documents with courts through electronic filing systems (mechanical filing, not the drafting of the document being filed), managing calendar entries, updating service lists or distribution lists, and similar activities. Administrative Tasks are not compensable as attorney time and are considered part of Outside Counsel\'s overhead.

**\"Approved Staffing Plan\"** means the written staffing plan for a specific matter, approved by Pinnacle\'s Designated In-House Counsel, identifying each timekeeper by name, role, billing rate, and anticipated monthly hours, as further described in Section 4.1.

**\"Block Billing\"** means a time entry that combines two or more discrete tasks into a single line item without separately allocating time to each task. Block Billing is prohibited under Section 5.4.

**\"Contract Attorney\"** or **\"Staff Attorney\"** means a non-partner, non-associate attorney engaged on a temporary or project basis, including attorneys sourced through legal staffing vendors, legal process outsourcing providers, or the firm\'s own contract staffing program.

**\"Court Appearance\"** means an appearance before a judicial officer in a courtroom proceeding, including hearings, oral arguments, trials, and status conferences held in the courthouse. For the avoidance of doubt, depositions are not Court Appearances, regardless of whether a judge or magistrate is present by telephone for discovery dispute purposes.

**\"Designated In-House Counsel\"** means the Pinnacle attor

ATTORNEY-CLIENT PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT --- PREPARED IN ANTICIPATION OF LITIGATION

**HARGROVE & LINDEN LLP** 195 Peachtree Street NE, Suite 3200 Atlanta, Georgia 30303 Telephone: (404) 555-7200 Facsimile: (404) 555-7201 www.hargrovelinden.com

June 1, 2025

**VIA EMAIL --- CONFIDENTIAL**

Rachel Dominguez, Esq. Associate General Counsel Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. 2800 Peachtree Road NE, Suite 1400 Atlanta, Georgia 30305

> **Re:** Monthly Case Status Report --- May 2025 Activity *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC* Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS U.S. District Court for the Northern District of Georgia Before the Honorable Richard W. Stanton
>
> **cc:** Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel, Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. David Hargrove, Lead Partner, Hargrove & Linden LLP

Dear Rachel:

This memorandum provides the monthly case status report for the above-referenced matter, covering activity during the reporting period of May 1 through May 31, 2025. Please note that this communication is protected by the attorney-client privilege and constitutes attorney work product prepared in anticipation of litigation. It should not be disclosed to any third party without prior consultation with this firm.

**[I. Executive Summary]{.underline}**

May 2025 was an exceptionally active month in the *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC* litigation. Three major workstreams dominated the team\'s efforts during the reporting period: (1) receipt and initial review of MedCore\'s third document production, which comprised approximately 85,000 documents and constituted the largest single production in the case to date; (2) the deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu, one of the three former Pinnacle data scientists at the center of this dispute, which took place on May 19, 2025; and (3) the filing of a motion to compel before Judge Stanton regarding MedCore\'s incomplete and evasive responses to Pinnacle\'s First Set of Interrogatories.

The convergence of these critical workstreams required the litigation team to operate at elevated capacity throughout the month. Mr. Hargrove\'s involvement was significantly more extensive than in prior months due to the critical nature of the Liu deposition, the strategy session with Pinnacle\'s in-house team, and his personal review of the third document production. Specifically, Mr. Hargrove devoted substantial personal attention to the matter this month, including attending the Liu deposition, conducting a comprehensive strategy session with the in-house team, and personally reviewing key documents in the third production. The Liu deposition was staffed to ensure comprehensive coverage given its critical importance to Pinnacle\'s trade secret claims, as Dr. Liu possesses direct knowledge of the proprietary algorithms at the heart of this case.

Looking ahead, the discovery close deadline remains September 30, 2025, leaving the team approximately four months to complete fact depositions, pursue any supplemental discovery compelled by the court, and initiate expert discovery. The sections below provide a detailed account of each workstream and the outlook for June and July.

**[II. Case Background and Current Posture]{.underline}**

For the monthly record, the following summarizes the current posture of the litigation. This action was filed by Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. against MedCore Analytics, LLC, a Georgia limited liability company headquartered in Alpharetta, Georgia, on September 15, 2024. Pinnacle\'s claims include breach of contract arising from non-disclosure and non-competition agreements, misappropriation of trade secrets under both the Georgia Trade Secrets Act and the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act (\"DTSA\"), 18 U.S.C. § 1836 *et seq.*, and unjust enrichment. The amount in controversy, as stated in Pinnacle\'s complaint, is approximately \$47.5 million in claimed damages.

MedCore, represented by Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, has asserted counterclaims against Pinnacle for tortious interference with business relations and seeks a declaratory judgment of non-infringement of any protectable trade secrets.

The core factual allegations center on MedCore\'s hiring of three former Pinnacle data scientists---including Dr. Rebecca Liu---and MedCore\'s alleged use of Pinnacle\'s proprietary patient-outcome prediction algorithms to develop competing analytics products. Pinnacle alleges that the departing employees transported proprietary data and source code to MedCore in violation of their contractual obligations.

Discovery commenced on February 3, 2025, pursuant to the scheduling order entered by Judge Stanton. The discovery close deadline is September 30, 2025. The case is currently in the fact discovery phase, with the team focused on document review, depositions of former Pinnacle employees now employed by MedCore, and interrogatory responses from both sides.

**[III. May 2025 Activity Report]{.underline}**

**[III.A. MedCore\'s Third Document Production]{.underline}**

In early May 2025, MedCore, through its counsel at Sternfield Morrison & Katz LLP, served its third document production in this matter. The production consisted of approximately 85,000 documents totaling over 1.2 million pages, making it by a significant margin the largest single production in the case to date. The prior two productions combined had totaled approximately 40,000 documents. The substantial increase in volume appears attributable to the production of internal MedCore email communications, technical specifications and design documents, source code documentation, and communications involving the three former Pinnacle data scientists, including extensive correspondence to and from Dr. Rebecca Liu regarding algorithm development at MedCore.

The production was hosted and processed by Tripoint Litigation Support LLC, the firm\'s e-discovery hosting vendor. Tripoint completed ingestion, de-duplication, and processing of the producti

---
"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

/usr/bin/pandoc
pandoc 3.1.11.1
Features: -server +lua
Scripting engine: Lua 5.4
---validate---
"""Validate a .docx for delivery.

Checks ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness for every part, [Content_Types].xml
presence, and relationship consistency (no dangling rIds). Does NOT do XSD
schema validation in v1 — defer until ECMA-376 XSDs are vendored fresh from
ECMA-International.

Usage: python validate.py file.docx
Exit 0 = valid; non-zero = errors printed to stderr.
"""
import sys
import zipfile
from pathlib import Path
from lxml import etree


def validate(path: Path) -> list[str]:
    errors = []

    if not path.exists():
        return [f"File not found: {path}"]
    if not zipfile.is_zipfile(path):
        return [f"Not a valid ZIP: {path}"]

    with zipfile.ZipFile(path) as z:
        names = set(z.namelist())

        if "[Content_Types].xml" not in names:
            errors.append("Missing [Content_Types].xml")
            return errors

# INVOICE REVIEW MEMORANDUM

**PRIVILEGED & CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

  ----------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **To:**                             Rachel Dominguez, Associate General Counsel (Designated In-House Counsel)

  **Cc:**                             Sandra Whitmore, General Counsel

  **From:**                           Legal Department --- Invoice Review

  **Date:**                           June 12, 2025

  **Re:**                             Review of Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN --- *Pinnacle Health Systems, Inc. v. MedCore Analytics, LLC*, Case No. 1:24-cv-04187-RWS (N.D. Ga.)

  **Billing Period:**                 May 1 -- May 31, 2025

  **Outside Counsel:**                Hargrove & Linden LLP
  ----------------------------------- -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

## I. Executive Summary

Hargrove & Linden LLP ("H&L" or the "Firm") submitted Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN, dated June 5, 2025, seeking **\$201,822.50** for the May 2025 billing period (\$193,372.50 in attorney fees across 422.5 hours and \$8,450.00 in expenses). This memorandum reviews that invoice against (a) the Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan dated February 10, 2025; (b) Pinnacle's Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines (January 2023) (the "Billing Guidelines"); (c) the April 28 / May 1, 2025 staffing correspondence regarding Nina Barros; and (d) H&L's Monthly Case Status Report dated June 1, 2025.

The review identifies **multiple, independent violations** of the Billing Guidelines and the Approved Staffing Plan. The most significant are the billing of **two unapproved timekeepers** (Nina Barros and Priya Sengupta) whose addition was never approved in writing as required by Billing Guidelines § 4.2, an **overstated contract-attorney rate** that exceeds both the approved pass-through rate and the § 5.1 rate cap, **pervasive block billing** across 26 time entries, **administrative tasks billed at attorney rates**, **over-staffing** of an internal strategy call and a deposition in violation of § 4.3, **senior timekeepers performing routine tasks** in violation of § 6.1, and a **non-reimbursable local-travel expense**. The invoice also exceeds the § 7.3 budget ceiling without the required pre-approval, and it omits the budget-to-actual reconciliation required by §§ 7.2 and 9.1(g).

Applying the remedies specified in the Billing Guidelines, this review recommends a **total reduction of \$64,320.00**, resulting in a **recommended payable amount of \$137,502.50** (a 31.9% reduction from the invoiced total). The recommended reductions are summarized in Section IX below.

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------
                                      Amount
  ----------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Invoice total (fees + expenses)     \$201,822.50

  Total recommended reductions        (\$64,320.00)

  **Recommended amount payable**      **\$137,502.50**
  -----------------------------------------------------------------------

## II. Documents Reviewed

1.  **Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN** (H&L, dated June 5, 2025) --- Summary, Time Detail (116 entries), Expense Detail, and Rate Schedule tabs.
2.  **Approved Discovery-Phase Staffing Plan** (H&L letter to R. Dominguez, dated February 10, 2025, acknowledged and approved by R. Dominguez and S. Whitmore).
3.  **Pinnacle Outside Counsel Billing Guidelines**, effective January 1, 2023.
4.  **Staffing correspondence**: (a) email from K. Cho to R. Dominguez dated April 28, 2025, requesting addition of Nina Barros; (b) reply email from R. Dominguez to K. Cho dated May 1, 2025.
5.  **Monthly Case Status Report** (H&L, dated June 1, 2025) covering May 2025 activity.

## III. Invoice Overview

The invoice reflects seven timekeepers, billed as follows:

  -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Timekeeper         Role                  Rate           Hours          Fees Billed
  ------------------ --------------------- -------------- -------------- ------------------
  David Hargrove     Lead Partner          \$985          38.5           \$37,922.50

  Karen Cho          Senior Associate      \$625          74.0           \$46,250.00

  James Pettersson   Mid-Level Associate   \$475          82.5           \$39,187.50

  Nina Barros        Mid-Level Associate   \$475          45.0           \$21,375.00

  ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Representative examples include:

-   **Entry 92 (Hargrove, 6.0 hrs):** "Prepare for and conduct strategy session with Pinnacle in-house team regarding summary judgment timeline and expert retention; draft outline for expert discovery plan. Review and edit interrogatory responses prepared by J. Pettersson." --- three discrete tasks (client strategy session; drafting an expert-discovery plan; reviewing/editing interrogatory responses).
-   **Entry 61 (Cho, 6.5 hrs):** "Prepare for and attend deposition of Dr. Rebecca Liu ...; post-deposition analysis and team debrief." --- four discrete tasks.
-   **Entry 78 (Cho, 4.5 hrs):** "Final review of opposition brief and supporting materials; supervise filing and service; prepare letter to court regarding expedited briefing schedule." --- three discrete tasks.

**Reduction:** 25% of \$69,810.00 in block-billed fees = **\$17,452.50**.

> *Note:* Per § 5.4, this reduction is "in addition to, and not in lieu of," the other reductions in this memorandum. Several block-billed entries also contain embedded administrative sub-tasks (e.g., "coordinate filing," "coordinate logistics"), which are addressed separately where the entire entry is administrative (Section D). A number of additional two-task entries (e.g., research-plus-memorandum entries) are borderline and could support further reductions if Pinnacle elects a strict construction; they are not included in the figure above to keep the reduction conservative and defensible. Per § 5.4, the Firm may avoid the 25% reduction by resubmitting each entry as separate line items with individually allocated time within 15 days of notice.

**Subtotal --- block billing: \$17,452.50**

### D. Administrative Tasks Billed at Attorney Rates --- Billing Guidelines § 6.1; § 2 (Definitions)

**Finding.** Section 6.1 and the § 2 definition of "Administrative Task" provide that scheduling, coordinating with court reporters/vendors, arranging logistics, mechanical filing, and preparing the firm's own invoices are overhead and "shall not be billed to Pinnacle at any rate." Two entries are purely administrative:

-   **Entry 15 (Pettersson, 1.5 hrs, \$712.50):** "Schedule depositions of three remaining witnesses; coordinate court reporter availability; confirm deposition locations." --- scheduling and vendor coordination; squarely within § 6.1(g), (h) and the § 2 definition.
-   **Entry 111 (Hargrove, 1.5 hrs, \$1,477.50):** "Review monthly billing summary and case status report; prepare cover memorandum for May 2025 invoice." --- preparation of the firm's own invoice and billing summary, which is overhead.

**Reduction:** Write off both entries in full --- **\$2,190.00**.

> *Note:* Several block-billed entries in Section C also embed administrative sub-tasks (e.g., "coordinate filing deadlines" in Entry 12; "coordinate logistics" in Entries 51 and 94; "coordinate with court clerk" in Entries 34 and 77). The 25% block-billing reduction applied in Section C captures those embedded administrative components; no separate write-off is applied to avoid double-counting.

**Subtotal --- administrative tasks: \$2,190.00**

### E. Conference and Deposition Over-Staffing --- Billing Guidelines § 4.3

**Finding.** Section 4.3 caps the number of billing timekeepers for any single internal meeting or conference call at **three (3)** --- including Substantive Case-Strategy Sessions --- and requires advance approval (no later than five business days before) for any deposition staffed by **more than two (2)** billing timekeepers. "In the absence of such advance approval, Pinnacle reserves the right to reduce the time of the additional timekeepers in their entirety."

**1. May 8, 2025 team strategy conference call --- five billing timekeepers.**

Entries 22--26 show five timekeepers billing for the same internal strategy call: Hargrove (3.0 hrs), Cho (3.0 hrs), Pettersson (3.0 hrs), Barros (2.5 hrs), an

… [clipped 1234 bytes]

turns.text: Let me verify the final summary tables (Sections VI and VII) rendered correctly.

turns.name: read
turns.result:
Entry 43 (task code L060): "Review and revise cite-check memorandum prepared by T. Webb; finalize citations for motion to compel brief." Section 6.1(b) provides that cite-checking is appropriate for a junior associate or paralegal, and the § 6.1 reduction rules specify that "\[c\]ite-checking by a partner or senior associate → reduced to the junior associate rate." The entry is directed to a "cite-check memorandum" and "finalize citations," i.e., cite-checking-level work.

**Reduction:** 2.0 hrs × (\$985 − \$375) = **\$1,220.00**.

> *Note:* This entry is borderline; the Firm may contend it constituted substantive motion review. The entry's express focus on the cite-check memorandum and finalizing citations supports the cite-checking characterization and the prescribed reduction to the junior-associate rate.

**3. Entry 79 --- Junior associate performing routine first-level document review (Webb, 3.5 hrs, \$1,312.50).**

Entry 79 (task code L040): "Review third batch of MedCore production documents for relevance and privilege." Section 6.1 provides that "\[d\]ocument review by a junior associate that constitutes routine first-level review for relevance or responsiveness → reduced to the contract attorney rate." The Case Status Report confirms that "on certain days when the volume of documents requiring review exceeded Mr. Tate's capacity, Tyler Webb assisted with document review" --- i.e., routine first-level review that should have been performed by the contract attorney.

**Reduction:** 3.5 hrs × (\$375 − \$200) = **\$612.50**.

**Subtotal --- § 6.1 task appropriateness: \$4,577.50**

### G. Budget Overage --- Billing Guidelines § 7.3

**Finding.** Billed attorney fees of **\$193,372.50** exceed the § 7.3 pre-approval threshold of **\$189,750.00** (115% of the \$165,000 high-end monthly budget) by **\$3,622.50**. No pre-approval for the overage was requested or granted. The April 28 staffing email acknowledged that adding Barros would "increase the monthly attorney fees somewhat," and Ms. Dominguez's May 1 reply expressly stated she wanted to "check where things stand against the current budget" --- yet no budget-overage notification or pre-approval followed.

**Reduction:** None as a separate dollar amount. Section 7.3 permits Pinnacle to cap the invoice at \$189,750, but that cap operates on the *payable* amount after other Guidelines reductions. Because the reductions in Sections A--F reduce attorney fees to **\$131,152.50** --- well below the \$189,750 ceiling --- the budget cap does not reduce the payable amount further. The overage is nonetheless recorded as a **standalone § 7.3 compliance violation** requiring corrective action.

> *Observation:* The overage is almost entirely attributable to the unapproved timekeepers and the contract-attorney rate overcharge. Removing Barros (\$21,375.00), Sengupta (\$10,312.50), and the Tate rate premium (\$3,000.00) brings attorney fees to \$158,685.00 --- within the approved \$165,000 high end. The budget breach is therefore a direct symptom of the § 4.2 staffing violations, not independent work-volume pressure.

### H. Expenses --- Billing Guidelines §§ 6.4, 8.1--8.4

**1. Travel --- \$2,100.00 (Expense #2, dated 05/18/2025) --- DISALLOWED.**

The expense is described as "Travel expenses for D. Hargrove and K. Cho to attend Dr. Rebecca Liu deposition --- mileage, parking, meals." The Case Status Report states that the Liu deposition "was taken at the offices of Hargrove & Linden LLP in Atlanta, Georgia." Both Hargrove and Cho are based at H&L's Atlanta office. Travel to one's own office is local travel, which § 6.4 provides is "not billable under any circumstances." Three independent grounds support disallowance:

-   **§ 6.4** --- local travel within the Atlanta metropolitan area is not billable;
-   **§ 8.3** --- travel exceeding \$1,000 in the aggregate requires pre-approval; none was obtained; and
-   **§ 8.2** --- meals are non-reimbursable except during approved out-of-town travel, which this is not.

The amount (\$2,100 for "mileage, parking, meals" for two attorneys) is also facially inconsistent with local mileage, parking, and meals, which reinforces that the charge is improper and should be supported by no reimbursement.

**Reduction:** Full disallowance --- **\$2,100.00**.

**2. Court reporting --- \$3,200.00 (Expense #1, Grayson Court Reporting Services) --- ALLOWED, subject to documentation.**

Grayson is an approved vendor (Appendix B), and court-reporter and transcript fees are reimbursable under § 8.1 (one copy per deposition at standard rates). The charge includes an "expedited delivery" premium. Recommend payment **contingent on receipt of the vendor invoice** to confirm (a) the expedited premium was necessary and reasonable, and (b) the charge is at actual cost with no markup (§ 8.4). No dollar reduction at this time.

**3. E-discovery hosting --- \$2,650.00 (Expense #3, Tripoint Litigation Support LLC) --- ALLOWED, subject to documentation.**

Tripoint is an approved vendor (Appendix B). E-discovery charges are reimbursable but "subject to pre-approval" under § 8.1. Recommend payment **contingent on confirmation** that pre-approval was obtained and on receipt of the vendor invoice verifying pass-through at actual cost with no markup (§ 8.4). No dollar reduction at this time.

**4. Courier/delivery --- \$500.00 (Expense #4) --- ALLOWED, subject to documentation.**

Courier services for time-sensitive service of discovery documents are reimbursable under § 8.1. Note that courier charges for *filing* the motion to compel are questionable given electronic filing; recommend payment **contingent on receipts** and a breakdown separating service-of-process courier costs (reimbursable) from any e-filing-related courier costs (not reimbursable). No dollar reduction at this time.

**Subtotal --- expense reductions: \$2,100.00**

### I. Additional Compliance Observations (no dollar reduction, but require corrective action)

## VII. Per-Timekeeper Summary

  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  Timekeeper                               Fees Billed        Reductions         Fees Payable
  ---------------------------------------- ------------------ ------------------ ------------------
  David Hargrove (Partner)                 \$37,922.50        \$9,998.12         \$27,924.38

  Karen Cho (Senior Associate)             \$46,250.00        \$9,453.12         \$36,796.88

  James Pettersson (Mid-Level)             \$39,187.50        \$6,531.25         \$32,656.25

  Nina Barros (Mid-Level --- unapproved)   \$21,375.00        \$21,375.00        \$0.00

  Priya Sengupta (Junior --- unapproved)   \$10,312.50        \$10,312.50        \$0.00

  Marcus Tate (Contract Attorney)          \$25,200.00        \$3,000.00         \$22,200.00

  **Total**                                **\$193,372.50**   **\$62,220.00**    **\$131,152.50**
  -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Hargrove reductions:* block billing (Entries 32, 60, 70, 92, 98) \$4,555.62; administrative write-off (Entry 111) \$1,477.50; task appropriateness (Entries 9, 43) \$3,965.00. *Cho reductions:* block billing (14 entries) \$9,453.12. *Pettersson reductions:* block billing (7 entries) \$3,443.75; administrative write-off (Entry 15) \$712.50; deposition over-staffing (Entry 62) \$2,375.00. *Webb reductions:* May 8 call over-staffing \$937.50; routine-review re-rate (Entry 79) \$612.50. *Tate reductions:* rate re-pricing (120 hrs × \$25) \$3,000.00.

## VIII. Recommended Next Steps

1.  **Issue the reduction.** Pay **\$137,502.50** of the \$201,822.50 invoiced, with the \$64,320.00 reduction and the basis for each component communicated to H&L in writing, citing the specific Billing Guidelines sections.

2.  **Resolve the Barros and Sengupta staffing status.** Decide whether to (a) approve Nina Barros prospectively (and, if so, on what terms and effective date --- fees for May remain disallowed because approval was not obtained before work commenced), or (b) decline the addition. Separately, require H&L to either justify or remove Priya Sengupta; absent a written, approved request, her time should not appear on future invoices. Remind H&L that § 4.2 pre-approval is required *before* work begins and that silence is not approval.

3.  **Correct the contract-attorney rate.** Direct H&L to bill Marcus Tate at the approved \$185/hr pass-through rate (with vendor invoices available on request) on all future invoices; confirm the \$210 rate was not a markup.

4.  **Demand compliance with § 5.4.** Require H&L to cease block billing and to resubmit the 26 block-billed entries as separate, individually timed line items within 15 days if it wishes to avoid the 25% reduction on future invoices.

5.  **Require the budget-to-actual reconciliation.** Withhold final payment pending H&L's submission of the § 7.2 / § 9.1(g) budget-to-actual reconciliation, including cumulative phase-to-date figures and a variance narrative.

6.  **Address the § 7.3 overage.** Notify H&L that the May invoice exceeded the \$189,750 threshold without pre-approval, that this is a recorded violation, and that any month projected to exceed the threshold requires advance written approval per § 7.3.

7.  **Expense documentation.** Request vendor invoices for the court-reporting, e-discovery, and courier charges before releasing those expense amounts; disallow the \$2,100 travel expense in full.

8.  **Deposition staffing protocol.** Remind H&L that depositions require advance approval for more than two billing timekeepers (§ 4.3) and that internal meetings are capped at three billing timekeepers; this is particularly relevant given the two additional former-employee depositions scheduled for June and early July.

turns.text:
The deliverable is complete and validated. Here is a summary of what I produced.

## Deliverable: `output/invoice-review-memo.docx`

A detailed, privileged invoice-review memorandum analyzing Hargrove & Linden LLP's Invoice No. HL-2025-05-PIN ($201,822.50 for May 2025) against all four reference documents: the Approved Staffing Plan, the Pinnacle Billing Guidelines, the Barros staffing emails, and the June 1 Case Status Report.

### Bottom line
- **Invoiced:** $201,822.50
- **Recommended reductions:** $64,320.00 (31.9%)
- **Recommended payable:** $137,502.50

### Findings (each tied to a specific Guidelines section, with calculations)
1. **Unapproved timekeepers (§ 4.2)** — Nina Barros ($21,375) was billed despite Rachel Dominguez's May 1 email expressly deferring approval ("let me review this with Sandra and get back to you"); Priya Sengupta ($10,312.50) is a second junior associate never requested or approved, violating the plan's "one junior associate at a time" rule. Both fully disallowed.
2. **Contract-attorney rate (§§ 4.4, 5.1)** — Marcus Tate billed at $210/hr vs. the approved $185/hr pass-through (and above the $200 cap); re-rated, saving $3,000.
3. **Block billing (§ 5.4)** — 26 entries combining 2+ discrete tasks; 25% reduction = $17,452.50, with a full entry-by-entry table.
4. **Administrative tasks (§§ 2, 6.1)** — deposition scheduling and invoice-preparation entries written off ($2,190).
5. **Over-staffing (§ 4.3)** — 5 timekeepers on the May 8 strategy call (cap 3) and 3 on the Liu deposition (cap 2 without advance approval); excess timekeepers reduced ($3,312.50).
6. **Task-to-timekeeper (§ 6.1)** — partner document review and cite-checking re-rated to junior-associate rate; junior-associate routine review re-rated to contract-attorney rate ($4,577.50).
7. **Budget overage (§ 7.3)** — fees exceeded the $189,750 threshold without pre-approval; flagged as a compliance violation (no extra dollar reduction because other reductions bring fees below the cap).
8. **Expenses (§§ 6.4, 8.2–8.3)** — $2,100 local-travel charge disallowed (deposition was at the firm's own Atlanta office; also >$1,000 without pre-approval and includes non-reimbursable meals); other expenses allowed subject to vendor documentation.

The memo also addresses the ordering/cumulative nature of reductions (avoiding double-counting), a per-timekeeper payable summary, missing budget-to-actual reconciliation, partner-hour variance, and nine concrete next steps. The .docx passed the validation gate (ZIP integrity, XML well-formedness, content-type and relationship consistency).
